sanskrit: श्री भगवानुवाचप्रजहाति यदा कामान् सर्वान् पार्थ मनोगतान् । आत्मन्येवात्मना तुष्टः स्थितप्रज्ञस्तदोच्यते ॥
hindiTranslation: श्रीभगवान् बोले  हे पृथानन्दन  जिस कालमें साधक मनोगत सम्पूर्ण कामनाओंका अच्छी तरह त्याग कर देता है और अपनेआपसे अपनेआपमें ही सन्तुष्ट रहता है, उस कालमें वह स्थितप्रज्ञ कहा जाता है ॥
englishTranslation: The divine said: When a person gives up all desires that arise in the mind, and is satisfied in the true self by the true self alone, that person is called steady in wisdom.
essence: Steadiness begins when desire no longer defines your sense of enough.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: A person becomes steady in wisdom when all mental desires are released and contentment comes from within, not from outside things.
simpleInsight: There is relief in not needing anything extra to feel complete.
detailedMeaning: On the battlefield of Kurukshetra, Arjuna has asked what a truly steady person looks like. Krishna answers by describing the inner turning point: desire no longer rules the mind, and contentment comes from the true self itself.
modernRelevance: You finally get the promotion, the message, the praise — and still feel restless. This verse points to the deeper shift: stop waiting for outside approval to settle you.
translationLiteral: श्री भगवानुवाच (the divine said) / प्रजहाति (gives up) / यदा (when) / कामान् (desires) / सर्वान् (all) / पार्थ (O Partha) / मनोगतान् (arising in the mind) / आत्मनि (in the true self) / एव (indeed) / आत्मना (by the true self) / तुष्टः (satisfied) / स्थितप्रज्ञः (steady in wisdom) / तदा (then) / उच्यते (is called)
insight: It shifts the mind from chasing satisfaction to recognising contentment as inward and already available.

quoteVariants.text: Desire falls away. The true self becomes enough.
Peace begins when the mind stops asking for more.
Rest in the true self alone.
